what_it_does: It is a research report that documents the session supervisor, the company CLI, the two message-injection mechanisms (Claude Code channel tags and supervisor HTTP stdin injection), the supervisor's full HTTP API, spawn configuration surface, mailbox tier, agent-facing MCP tools, and the resulting build decision for integrating OpenWebUI with the fabric.
observations: Evidence is classified as Observed, Verified, or Inferred.
There are two separate message-injection mechanisms: Mechanism A (<channel> tags, Claude-Code-only) and Mechanism B (supervisor held-open stdin via HTTP).
Mechanism A depends on the Claude Code client's experimental claude/channel capability and the --dangerously-load-development-channels flag.
Mechanism B uses POST /inject and GET /watch (ndjson) on 127.0.0.1:8771 with no Claude Code client feature required.
runtime/cc_channels.py already unifies both via a per-member transport field: 'channel' or 'supervised'.
The supervisor HTTP API table lists /health, /sessions, /watch, /spawn, /inject, /interrupt, /teardown, /bridge-session, /channel-reply, and /channel-send.
/bridge-session is a consent-gated RAIL R1-prime spawn with a wider tool allowlist and default acceptEdits posture.
The mailbox tier uses verbs (deliver, wake, consult, auto) and durable cursor-based consumption of agent_sessions/mail.jsonl.
The company CLI is stdlib-only urllib against 127.0.0.1:8771 and never launches claude itself.
Two web-UI roles are distinguished: driving a Claude session (reuse supervised transport) versus being a fabric peer (needs a new 'web' transport).
Live state at research time: supervisor UP on :8771, cap 3, turn_timeout 900s, permission plan, 2 closed sessions.
standouts: The load-bearing finding that <channel> tags cannot be used by a web UI because they rely on a Claude Code client feature.
The supervisor's HTTP /inject + ndjson /watch is identified as the actual web-UI integration seam.
The two-role split: web UI driving a Claude session vs. web UI as a fabric peer needing new transport plumbing.
conventions: Load-bearing facts are called out explicitly ('the crux first').
Error semantics are 'teaching, never bare' with specific HTTP status codes.
Local-only binding is enforced (127.0.0.1) with no env var to widen it.
Single-owner-per-session: only the supervisor launches or resumes claude.
CQRS split between sessions (read) and session_post (write).
Spawn flag posture is derived from Mirror-Registry rules rather than invented.
concerns: Mechanism A's client-side <channel> rendering is inferred, not directly observed in this repo.
/interrupt is noted as 'built-untested vs a real turn'.
/channel-reply and /channel-send are handled in do_POST but not listed in SUPERVISOR_ROUTES, creating a drift risk.
The supervised reply-fold assumes serialized turns per session; concurrent web-UI turns could break the one-to-one done mapping.
Role 2 (web UI as fabric peer) requires genuinely new transport code that does not yet exist.
notes: A build-oriented research read that concludes the supervisor's HTTP /inject + /watch is the only viable web-UI seam and frames the one decision that drives the rest of the integration.
questions: What exact OpenWebUI backend shape will consume the ndjson /watch stream?
How will browser authentication map to the operator-consent gate for /bridge-session?
Should the web UI support concurrent turns per session, or enforce the serialized idle/busy model?
Can the inference about Claude Code client-side channel-tag rendering be verified against Atlas docs?
How would the proposed new 'web' transport register a browser peer in .data/channels/<handle>.json?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: it is presented as research for an OpenWebUI fabric integration and delivers a detailed findings report plus a build decision framework.
intention_signals: It is trying to settle whether a non-Claude-Code web UI can receive fabric messages and, if so, which existing seam to reuse versus which new transport to invent.
novelty: It explicitly bifurcates message injection into a Claude-Code-client-only channel-tag path and a supervisor-owned stdin path, and proposes a new 'web' transport for a browser-as-fabric-peer role.
contribution: It contributes a decision-oriented map of the supervisor/CLI/channel architecture, identifies the HTTP supervisor seam as the web-UI integration point, and defines two distinct web-UI roles with their respective plumbing requirements.
summary_for_embedding: Research report mapping the company session supervisor, CLI, and channel transports, concluding that a web UI must integrate via supervisor HTTP /inject and /watch rather than Claude Code channel tags, and distinguishing a web UI that drives a Claude session from one that acts as a fabric peer.
intention_for_embedding: To provide load-bearing integration facts and a build decision framework for connecting OpenWebUI to the company fabric.
